nativeRange.habitat: Rocky reefs at depths of 2–15 m; inhabits empty barnacles and worm or mollusc tubes on rocky reefs in shallow tropical waters.

diet.notes: Feeds primarily on zooplankton suspended in the water column in the wild. In captivity tends to accept meaty frozen foods including mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, and finely chopped crustacean flesh. Small frequent feedings are generally recommended.

avoidWith: fish that compete heavily for the same food column
other barnacle blennies unless ample hiding spots provided

imageCandidates.notes: D Ross Robertson (STRI); 640x436. Striking head-on shot of the blenny peering from a barnacle hole — iridescent blue-spotted head, bold yellow-ringed ocellate eye, red lips and gill area, dark body blotches, cirri visible. Sharp, vivid, the classic barnacle-blenny pose; best representative image.

D Ross Robertson; 640x270. Full lateral whole-body view on black — shows red saddle spots along the flank, blue-edged fins, cirri and colouration clearly; strong diagnostic complement to the hero shot.

D Ross Robertson; 526x382. In-situ shot of the fish emerging from algae/urchin cover, head and forebody visible with iridescent spotting and ocellate eye; natural crevice-dwelling behaviour, good variety.

summary: The Mexican Barnacle Blenny is a small reef-associated fish from Mexico's Gulf of California, reaching around 5 cm. Generally considered reef-safe and peaceful, it tends to occupy a rock crevice or barnacle shell, darting out to intercept passing zooplankton.
careNotes: The Mexican Barnacle Blenny has been reported to spend the majority of its time tucked inside a rock crevice, barnacle shell, or hollow tube, darting out rapidly to feed on passing zooplankton. A well-established aquarium with abundant rockwork and hiding places tends to be essential for this species to thrive. It generally does best with small, non-aggressive tank mates; moderately aggressive companions have been known to prevent this fish from getting adequate food. While peaceful toward other species, it can be territorial toward conspecifics if suitable perch sites are limited — solo housing or multiple individuals with ample individual territories are generally advised. Feeding with small, frequent portions of frozen mysis or brine shrimp broadcast into the water column is commonly recommended, as this fish feeds in a planktivorous manner despite its carnivore classification.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (6.0 cm TL), temperature range (23.3–29.1°C preferred), native range (Eastern Central Pacific, southern Baja California to Acapulco and Revillagigedo Islands), and taxonomy (Chaenopsidae, Blenniiformes). FishBase adult size slightly larger than LiveAquaria's 2-inch figure; averaged to 3.8–5.5 cm. FishBase temperature max (29.1°C) higher than LiveAquaria (25.6°C); averaged with Reeflings to produce 23–28°C range.

additional.name: Reeflings
additional.notes: Third source cross-check. Confirmed adult size ~5 cm, reef safe, peaceful temperament. Temperature 24–28°C and SG 1.022–1.025 consistent with FishBase and LiveAquaria after averaging. Care level listed as 'easy/beginner' — disagreed with LiveAquaria 'moderate'; intermediate used as average.

additional.name: reefguide.org
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size up to 2 in (5 cm) and native range (Gulf of California, Pacific Coast of Mexico). Limited husbandry data; used for range confirmation only.
